---
title: "World History"
description: "A speculative timeline from 2050 to 2150: augmented reality, Esosense, the collapse of MERIDIAN, and a Privacy Rights Management system built into human biology."
author: synesis
date: "2026-04-25"
categories: [sci-fi, fiction, privacy, personal]
image: cover.jpg
---

![A crowd under night lights.](cover.jpg){fig-align="center"}

*Originally written in 2014. Revised April 2026.*

---

### 2050 — Esosense

[Augmented reality](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Augmented_reality) had become widespread decades earlier, replacing traffic lights, building signs, and eventually most of the visual and auditory signage humans once relied on to navigate the world. But by 2050, the wearables that delivered it were no longer enough. A "naked" person simply could not survive in the brave new world.

A new device was born: an organic implant, inserted into newborns by legal mandate. Its bio circuitry would grow in stages with the human body, eventually becoming an integral part of the sensory system: sight, hearing, spatial awareness, communication, all mediated through a single substrate.

It was called **Esosense**.

### 2090 — The Collapse

The global clandestine monitoring system **MERIDIAN** was hacked. It was never determined who was behind it or how long it had been going on. The hackers exploited everything the system had collected: personal communications, financial transactions, internal records of every government and corporation, control systems for critical infrastructure, and, thanks to Esosense, the physical location of every human being on the planet.

The result was catastrophic. Financial and political systems were manipulated for the gain of a few. Economies were decimated. Riots and wars erupted. Half the world's population was gone.

### 2110 — The Federated Nations

Two decades later, the surviving geopolitical entities formed a central governance body, the **Federated Nations (FN)**, with a single founding mandate: *complete privacy protection*. Never again would centralized surveillance be possible.

Esosense, the very technology that had made the catastrophe possible, became the foundation of the solution. At implantation, a [public/private key pair](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Public-key_cryptography) would be embedded deep inside each newborn's bio circuitry. The public key would be broadcast to nearby Esosense hosts and propagated peer-to-peer across the global mesh, with no central registry and no single point of compromise. Within hours of birth, every living person's Esosense would hold the new key. The key would become a person's identity for life, and the ultimate mechanism for controlling who could *observe* them.

### 2120 — Privacy Rights Management

The system that emerged was called **PRM (Privacy Rights Management)**. It worked like this:

Every person's Esosense continuously broadcast an encrypted "bill of rights" over short range, specifying their willingness to be observed: visually, acoustically, by smell, by touch. The broadcast carried an ephemeral proximity token rather than a stable identifier, so passive listeners could not build long-term maps of who was near whom. Once consent was resolved, the receiver's Esosense *mediated* all sensory input at the signal stage, before conscious perception. If person A did not consent to being seen by person B, then B's Esosense substituted A's face with a plausible but non-persistent placeholder before the image ever reached B's visual cortex. B would perceive a person, not an uncanny shape, but would have no stable features to remember and no way to re-identify A on a later encounter. The same filtering applied to every sense.

The processing happened entirely inside the head, powered by [biological computation](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Wetware_computer) circuits that had matured over the preceding century, vastly different in form from their silicon forebears.

Indirect observation (cameras, microphones, [DNA profiling](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/DNA_profiling)) followed the same logic. The observing device would decrypt, check consent, and anonymize at the point of capture. No non-sanctioned observation would ever be recorded or transmitted. Legacy equipment from the pre-FN era was a separate problem with a simpler solution: it had been decommissioned during the regime's first decade, and the networks that once transmitted its output no longer existed.

The attack surface was not ignored. A **peer-to-peer authentication protocol** verified that every Esosense instance and every observing device was running a genuine, untampered PRM implementation. Any detected tampering triggered an immediate response: for devices manufactured under the new regime, that response was **self-termination**.

For devices, this meant shutdown. For Esosense, which by 2120 was an inseparable part of the human nervous system, the implications were left unspoken in the FN's published specifications. But everyone understood.

### 2130 — The Last Loophole

A subtler problem remained: DNA. Every human body sheds cells constantly, and a century of progress in genetics had made it trivial to identify someone from a stray skin cell. PRM could mediate what you *saw* and *heard*, but it could not follow every cell that left your body.

The solution was biological. A cellular mechanism was engineered to destroy every identifying biomolecule in any cell that detached from the body. The trigger was loss of a continuous signaling molecule maintained only in living, [perfused](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Perfusion) tissue. Once a cell was shed and the signal dropped, a cascade of [nucleases](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Nuclease) and [proteases](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Protease) would activate, cutting DNA and RNA into unidentifiable fragments and breaking proteins down to their constituent amino acids within minutes. What remained was generic biochemical residue. Your biology, like your face and voice, had become private by default.

### 2150 — The Unasked Question

By 2150, a generation had grown up under PRM. They had never seen an unfiltered face without consent. They had never been observed without choosing to be. Privacy was no longer a right to be defended. It had become the substrate of experience itself.

But an observation circulated in certain corners, never quite spoken aloud in polite company. The protocol was peer-to-peer; the keys were distributed; no data center held the record of who was who. What had been centralized was subtler: a single implementation, installed in every nervous system at birth, held uniform by a kill switch that made non-compliance fatal. Whoever controlled the specification controlled eight billion nervous systems. Was this privacy, or was it the most complete form of control ever devised?

The FN's answer, when pressed, was always the same: the system is open. The protocol is auditable. The keys are distributed. No one entity controls it.

That was true, for the moment. But the architecture offered no guarantee it would remain true. One could read the protocol. One could not *opt out* of it. The physical world had been stripped bare decades ago: no signs, no signals, no navigable infrastructure visible to the unaugmented eye. A person without Esosense could not cross a street, enter a building, or be recognized as a citizen. And a person whose Esosense detected tampering would not survive at all.

The architects of PRM had set out to build the opposite of MERIDIAN. Whether they had succeeded, or merely built its mirror image, was a question the system itself was not designed to answer.

---

*To be continued...*
